title: ConsecutiveLossRule never computes consecutive loss history

evidence.quote: The method has a value receiver, checks only LastTrade, mutates currentConsecutive on the copy, then compares it to the threshold.
The test notes that state does not persist and only verifies the method exists and runs.
The winning-trade case also only calls IsSatisfied without asserting the result.

reasoning: A freshly constructed rule with MaxConsecutiveLosses greater than one can only increment its copied counter to one per call, regardless of how many losing trades already exist. Repeated calls can also count the same last loss multiple times instead of distinct trades.
reproduction: After recording three closed losing trades, NewConsecutiveLossRule(3).IsSatisfied(0, record) returns false instead of true.
recommendation: Compute the consecutive losing suffix directly from record.Trades on each call, or use pointer state that tracks the last processed trade to avoid recounting the same trade.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: The tests explicitly avoid asserting the result for consecutive loss scenarios, so the known non-persistent state bug is not caught.
suggestedRegressionTest: Assert three consecutive losing closed trades satisfy a threshold of three, and assert two losses followed by one winning trade does not satisfy it.
